answer:
**The poem does not explicitly name anyone who taught "you" (the addressee—a kitten, playfully styled as the "beloved") the art of stolen glances.** The opening lines pose this as a rhetorical question to the kitten, observing its flirtatious eye movements and pawing, before pivoting to a philosophical reflection that love and the sense of beauty are innate and universal, present in all creation rather than taught by any specific figure.[^1][^2]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 26: "Tujh ko duzdida nigahi yeh sikha di kis ne" ([paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/26-tujh-ko-duzdida-nigahi-yeh-sikha-di-kis-ne/translation/#p-4ab922)).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 26: "Tujh ko duzdida nigahi yeh sikha di kis ne" ([passage](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/26-tujh-ko-duzdida-nigahi-yeh-sikha-di-kis-ne/translation/#p-4ab922-p-69ebd5)).
